metadata.summary: Message queue reliability: publisher confirms, DLQ with alerting, exponential backoff with jitter, backpressure via prefetch limits, schema versioning, and replay runbooks for production incidents.

content.title: Message Queue Reliability
content.summary: Reliable message queues require four complementary practices: publisher confirms so "published" means durably stored (not just buffered); DLQ with alerting so poison messages are isolated rather than lost silently; exponential backoff with jitter so retry storms do not DOS the broker; and backpressure via prefetch limits so slow consumers do not starve siblings or OOM the broker.

why.p: The most common failure mode is silent data loss. A producer that publishes without confirms only knows the message reached the broker's TCP buffer. If the broker crashes before writing to disk, the message is gone. A consumer that nacks-to-DLQ without an alert produces a pile of failed messages that nobody notices until a business process stops working.

The second most common failure is retry storms. A broker hiccup triggers simultaneous retries from all consumers. Without backoff and jitter, all retries land at the same instant, overwhelm the broker, and extend the outage.

list.item: Any queue that carries business-critical messages where loss means a lost order, payment, or user action.
Any queue with a retry policy — applies backoff and jitter rules.
Any queue that routes to a DLQ — applies alerting rules.
Any schema that changes — applies versioning rules.
Fire-and-forget logging pipelines where occasional message loss is acceptable — publisher confirms and DLQs add overhead without value.
Ephemeral event streams for real-time dashboards where stale data is simply not displayed — at-most-once delivery is correct.

rule.statement: Producers MUST use publisher confirms (RabbitMQ) or acks=all (Kafka) before treating a message as published. Without confirms, "published" means "written to the local TCP send buffer" — a broker crash before flush loses the message with no indication of failure.
rule.rationale: RabbitMQ publisher confirms are asynchronous broker acks sent after the message is written to the durable queue. Kafka acks=all requires the leader and all in-sync replicas to acknowledge. Without either, the message is acknowledged by the broker's TCP stack, not its storage engine.

example.title: RabbitMQ publisher confirms
example.note: With confirm_delivery() enabled, basic_publish raises NackError if the broker cannot accept the message durably. Catch and handle — do not silently swallow.

example.title: Kafka producer with acks=all
example.note: enable.idempotence=True activates Kafka's producer-side exactly-once. It requires acks=all and retries > 0. Without it, network retries can produce duplicates even with acks=all.

rule.statement: Every queue that can receive a failure MUST have a DLQ configured before going live, and DLQ depth MUST trigger an alert when non-zero for more than 5 minutes. A DLQ without an alert is equivalent to no DLQ.
rule.rationale: Without a DLQ, failed messages either retry forever (blocking the queue and consuming worker capacity) or are silently lost (nack without requeue). Without an alert, the DLQ becomes a graveyard — messages accumulate unnoticed while the business process stops working.

rule.statement: Every DLQ deployment MUST include a replay runbook (script or documented procedure) that: reads messages from the DLQ, applies the fix, republishes to the main queue, and deletes from the DLQ. The runbook must be tested before the queue goes live.
rule.rationale: A DLQ without a replay path is a write-only dead end. The messages carry the evidence of what failed; the replay is how you recover. Teams that skip the runbook discover they cannot replay safely under production pressure.

antipattern.description: DLQ with no alert and no replay runbook — the common "set it and forget it" configuration.
antipattern.reason: DLQ messages are production incidents in slow motion. Without an alert, no one knows they are accumulating. Without a runbook, the replay is ad-hoc and risky. Both are required before a queue goes live.

rule.statement: Retry policies MUST specify: max retries (hard ceiling), initial backoff interval, backoff multiplier, maximum backoff interval, and jitter (randomization ±20-50% of the computed delay). Infinite retry without a DLQ ceiling is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Without a hard ceiling, a poison message retries forever, consumes a worker, and blocks the queue. Without jitter, all consumers retrying after a broker hiccup fire simultaneously at the same instant, producing a thundering-herd that extends the outage.

example.title: Exponential backoff with jitter
example.note: Distinguish TransientError (retry) from TerminalError (skip to DLQ immediately). Retrying a validation error or a permanent 400 response wastes retries and delays DLQ routing.

antipattern.description: Naive while True retry loop without backoff or ceiling on broker hiccup.
antipattern.reason: All consumers retry simultaneously with zero delay, flooding the broker with requests at the exact moment it is recovering. This produces a retry storm that extends the outage. Use exponential backoff with jitter and a hard retry ceiling.

rule.statement: Set prefetch_count (RabbitMQ) or max.poll.records (Kafka) to limit the number of in-flight messages per consumer. A slow consumer without a prefetch limit gobbles the queue and starves sibling consumers, concentrating all unprocessed messages on one worker.
rule.rationale: Without prefetch limits, a broker dispatches all available messages to the first available consumer. If that consumer is slow or fails, all messages are blocked. Prefetch_count=10 is a safe starting point; tune based on message processing time and consumer count.

rule.statement: Set queue length limits on the broker (RabbitMQ x-max-length, SQS MessageRetentionPeriod, Kafka log.retention.bytes) to prevent OOM when producers outpace consumers. Combine with producer-side acks to propagate backpressure to the producer.
rule.rationale: Producers without flow control fill brokers indefinitely. A queue with no length limit and a slow consumer is a time bomb. Length limits force the system to shed load visibly rather than silently accumulating unbounded backlogs.

rule.statement: Message schemas MUST be registered in a schema registry (Avro, Protobuf, or JSON Schema) and versioned. Consumers MUST be backward-compatible across at least one schema version. Adding optional fields is allowed without a version bump; removing or renaming existing fields requires a new version with a migration fence.
rule.rationale: Schema drift is the most common source of deserialization failures in production. Without a registry, agents add fields without bumping the version, old consumers crash on the unknown field, and the failure cascades silently to the DLQ before anyone notices.

antipattern.description: Adding a required field to a message schema without a version bump and consumer migration.
antipattern.reason: Old consumers that do not know the new required field fail on every message after the schema change. Deploy consumer first (backward-compatible, ignores new field), then deploy producer that sends the new field, then bump the schema version and make the field required.

section.name: monitoring
section.p: Minimum observable signals for any production queue:

list.item: Queue depth (messages waiting) — growing depth indicates consumer lag or crash.
Consumer lag (Kafka: offset lag per partition) — leading indicator of throughput issues.
DLQ size — non-zero for more than 5 minutes triggers PagerDuty/alert.
Oldest unacked message age — indicates a consumer stuck on a poison message.
Processing error rate (messages nacked / messages consumed) — baseline for SLO.
Message rate (in/out) — detect sudden spikes or drops in producer volume.

checklist.check: Publisher confirms or acks=all enabled on every producer.
DLQ configured on every queue before go-live.
Alert on DLQ depth > 0 for more than 5 minutes.
DLQ replay runbook written and tested.
Retry policy specifies max retries, backoff, multiplier, max delay, and jitter.
Prefetch count or max.poll.records set on every consumer.
Queue length limit set on broker to prevent unbounded growth.
Schema registered in a registry with version; consumers backward-compatible by one version.
Metrics: queue depth, consumer lag, DLQ size, oldest-unacked-age, error rate, message rate.

section.name: exactly-once-reality
section.p: Exactly-once delivery semantics: what brokers actually provide and what the application must add.

rule.statement: Do NOT rely on broker-advertised exactly-once delivery without verifying all required configurations are active: Kafka requires enable.idempotence=true on the producer, a transactional producer session, and isolation.level=read_committed on the consumer. RabbitMQ requires publisher confirms plus application-level deduplication. A single missing config silently reverts to at-least-once.
rule.rationale: Exactly-once is the hardest guarantee in distributed systems. All major brokers that claim it require specific, non-default configuration on both sides of the pipe. Agents frequently enable one side but not the other, producing silent duplicates in production.

section.p: Practical recommendation: build idempotent consumers (see mq-idempotent-consumers) and accept at-least-once. This is more portable, simpler to reason about, and survives broker upgrades that change EOS behavior.
